summary: DeVault argues that the term 'open source' has a specific, well-established meaning defined by the Open Source Initiative's Open Source Definition, and that companies misusing the term to describe source-available software are engaging in dishonest, anti-social behavior. He draws an analogy to plastic industry 'Resin Identification Codes' that mimic the recycling symbol to deceive consumers. The core of his argument is that open source software belongs to its community of contributors, not just its publisher, and therefore the community's right to commercialize it is fundamental and non-negotiable. He contends that companies seeking commercial monopolies over their projects use 'motivated reasoning' to redefine open source in self-serving ways. DeVault concludes that publishers can choose any license terms they wish, but calling non-open-source software 'open source' is wrong when better terms like 'source available' exist.
key_points: Open source software is collectively owned by its community of contributors, not just its publisher, both morally and legally through retained copyrights
Companies redefining 'open source' to mean merely 'source code you can read' are engaging in motivated reasoning to justify commercial monopolies over community-built software
The open source brand is one of the most successful in the software industry, which is exactly why dishonest actors want to co-opt it
CLAs are explicitly described as promises to eventually make projects proprietary, and should never be signed
The plastic industry's deceptive Resin Identification Codes serve as an apt analogy for how the open source label gets exploited
money_quotes: The software does not belong to its publisher, but to its community.

Because it is the product of its community as a whole, the community enjoys the right to commercialize it, without limitation. This is a fundamental, non-negotiable part of the open source definition.
Their real reason is 'I should be able to make money from open source, but you shouldn't'.
When you get down to it, this behavior is dishonest and anti-social.
The open source brand is incredibly strong — one of the most successful brands in the entire software industry.
If it's not open source, it's wrong to call it open source.
A CLA is an explicit promise that the steward of an open source project will pull the rug out later and make the project proprietary. Never sign a CLA.
key_insight: Misappropriating the 'open source' label for source-available software is a form of brand exploitation that undermines the community rights the term was built to protect.
